us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes, Refuses to Rule Out Ground Troops
us-politics.body:
President Trump escalated pressure on Iran over Easter weekend, posting a profanity-laced threat to bomb power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened. He declined to rule out ground troops if Tehran refuses a deal, though a House Republican said boots on the ground would serve only one purpose - seizing enriched uranium.

Reactions split sharply across party lines. Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with Trump, calling the threats "insane." Top Armed Services Democrat Adam Smith warned a war with Iran would be a costly mistake. A former Trump adviser said the aggressive rhetoric makes a negotiated deal less likely, not more.

House Democrats argued Iran's grip on the Strait of Hormuz poses a greater strategic threat than its nuclear program. NATO allies are watching closely as Trump's posture strains alliance relationships.

sources.title: House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened

us-politics.headline: DHS Funding Lapse Deepens as GOP Divisions Stall Reopening
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded as House Republicans struggle to agree on a path forward during recess. Internal GOP divisions and what sources describe as Trump's detachment from the negotiations have stymied efforts to resolve the lapse.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries publicly called on Speaker Mike Johnson to cut the recess short and return members to Washington. Senator Tim Kaine used the moment to warn that the firing of Attorney General Pam Bondi should serve as a cautionary lesson for whoever Trump nominates next.

Trump told reporters not to expect a broader Cabinet shake-up following the departures of Bondi and Kristi Noem. Democrats and some Republicans are pressing for action, but no vote is currently scheduled.

sources.title: Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS
Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Draws Fire on NASA Cuts and Defense Spending
us-politics.body:
Trump's proposed federal budget is facing scrutiny from both parties. NASA's administrator stepped forward to defend deep proposed cuts to the agency, framing them as a necessary realignment toward priorities like lunar exploration - though critics argue the reductions undermine long-term American competitiveness in space.

Rep. Madeleine Dean used a weekend interview to challenge the budget's overall direction, linking its defense spending assumptions to the escalating Iran situation. Maryland Governor Wes Moore argued separately that ending foreign wars would do more to lower gas prices than any domestic energy policy shift.

An opinion piece in The Hill warned that Democrats risk political damage if they fail to develop a credible affordability message before the midterms, suggesting the budget fight could become a defining electoral battleground.

sources.title: Rep. Madeleine Dean discusses Iran and President Trump's proposed budget
Moore: Ending foreign wars ‘best thing that we could do’ to lower gas prices
NASA administrator defends Trump’s proposed budget cuts
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum
The war in Iran is showing why the US needs diverse energy resources
The White House vows America will never give up the moon again

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028 as Khanna and Gallego Make Early Moves
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said his party must "look at" a potential 2028 presidential run, stopping short of a formal declaration but signaling serious interest. His comments come as Democrats search for a post-Biden identity and a candidate capable of winning back working-class voters.

Rep. Ro Khanna pushed back against Democrats who argue the party should avoid engaging with unconventional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker. Khanna said that reluctance to meet voters where they are will cost the party future elections. The debate reflects a broader internal argument about outreach strategy and whether traditional media appearances are enough.

sources.title: Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Reshapes Australian Commuting and Energy Debate
au-politics.body:
Soaring fuel prices are pushing Australians toward alternatives at speed. E-bike retailers report surging sales as commuters ditch cars, while used electric vehicle prices are climbing as demand outstrips supply. Petrol station owners, caught between squeezed margins and angry customers, are questioning whether their businesses remain viable.

The crisis has a global dimension. Energy Minister Chris Bowen has called for the Strait of Hormuz to reopen after Iran began charging vessels transit fees - one ship reportedly paid US$2 million to pass. Trump has threatened to bomb Iranian power plants in response to the standoff, raising fears of further supply disruption. Australia, heavily exposed to global oil markets, faces continued price pressure if the strait remains contested.

sources.title: Australia news live: Moira Deeming secures top spot on Victorian Liberal ticket; Chris Bowen calls for Hormuz reopening
Commuters are switching from cars to e-bikes to save money on fuel
Price crunch has some servo owners asking: 'Is it really worth doing this?'
One ship paid US$2m to pass Iran. Now we're looking at a new era
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits
Live: Trump threatens to bomb Iran power plants in expletive-laden post

au-politics.headline: Victorian Liberals Back Deeming; Rights Commission Probes NSW Discrimination
au-politics.body:
Moira Deeming has secured the top spot on the Victorian Liberal Senate ticket, a result that cements her position within the party after a period of internal conflict with former leader Matthew Guy. Her preselection signals the party's conservative wing retains significant influence heading into the federal election.

Separately, the Australian Human Rights Commission has launched an investigation into alleged racial discrimination by the NSW government. Details of the specific conduct under scrutiny remain limited, but the probe adds to ongoing national debate about institutional treatment of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people - a debate also reflected in a family court ruling ordering an Aboriginal child returned to their remote NT community after being moved 1,700 kilometres away.

sources.title: Australia news live: Moira Deeming secures top spot on Victorian Liberal ticket; Chris Bowen calls for Hormuz reopening
Australian Human Rights Commission investigates alleged racial discrimination by NSW government
Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Youth Justice Cells Condemned; Aboriginal Rangers Tackle Biosecurity
au-politics.body:
A government watchdog has called for urgent upgrades to youth detention cells it describes as harmful, warning current conditions obstruct access to justice. The findings add pressure on state governments already facing scrutiny over youth crime policy, with critics and advocates divided on whether the priority should be rehabilitation or stronger deterrence.

On country, a national conference of young Aboriginal land and sea rangers focused on practical challenges including feral animal control and biosecurity threats. Rangers play a frontline role in managing invasive species across remote Australia, and the conference highlighted growing demand for structured career pathways in the sector.

sources.title: Watchdog seeks upgrade to 'harmful' youth cells to improve access to justice
Feral animals, biosecurity and careers on agenda for young Aboriginal rangers

au-politics.headline: Swift Parrot Habitat Logged; Bat Deaths Follow Cyclone Narelle
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest where the critically endangered swift parrot was recorded calling dozens of times has been logged, raising fresh questions about the effectiveness of environmental protections for threatened species. The swift parrot is considered one of Australia's most at-risk birds, and conservationists say the incident exposes gaps between survey findings and forestry decisions.

In the Northern Territory, wildlife rescuers are responding to mass deaths of baby flying foxes following Cyclone Narelle. Hundreds of juvenile bats have been found dying near Katherine and Pine Creek, with carers stretched thin. The losses come as bilby populations show encouraging growth inside predator-free sanctuaries, offering a rare conservation bright spot over the Easter period.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged
Wildlife rescuers scramble as hundreds of baby bats lie dying in outback
'Easter bilby' populations booming across sanctuaries

world.headline: Iran-Israel War Escalates as Trump Threatens Hormuz Consequences
world.body:
The conflict between Israel and Iran has widened sharply. Iranian missiles struck a residential building in Haifa and an industrial zone in southern Israel, while Israel launched retaliatory air strikes on Iranian targets and continued pounding Lebanon, killing at least 14 people there. A US airman was rescued from a remote part of Iran during the fighting, with Trump declaring victory - though analysts warn significant threats to US operations remain.

Trump followed the rescue with an expletive-laden social media post threatening Iran with 'hell' if it moves to block the Strait of Hormuz. BBC reporters near the strait described conditions on the edge of a battlefield. Iran's internet has been shut down throughout the conflict, now breaking records as the longest wartime blackout the country has seen.

sources.title: 'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Trump issues expletive-laden threat  to Iran over Hormuz Strait blockage
Analysis: Trump declares victory in Iran war after rescue, but threats to US operation still loom
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches

world.headline: Gaza Deteriorates as Hamas Rejects Disarmament and Strikes Continue
world.body:
Hamas's armed wing, the Qassam Brigades, flatly rejected demands to disarm, calling them unacceptable while Israeli air strikes continued across Gaza City. One strike hit a crowd, with footage circulating widely. Israel is simultaneously conducting operations in Gaza, Lebanon, and against Iran - a multi-front campaign drawing growing international criticism.

Conditions inside Gaza are worsening fast. Tent camps are overrun with rodent infestations, aid access remains severely restricted, and Christians marked Easter in mourning rather than celebration. Israel's far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir outlined plans for a Palestinian 'death row', adding to alarm over the direction of Israeli policy. The Pope used Easter to warn that the world is growing indifferent to violence.

sources.title: Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russia-Linked Gas Pipeline in Central Europe
world.body:
Serbia and Hungary both reported discovering explosives planted at a Russian gas pipeline running through the region. Hungary separately alleged a plot to blow up the infrastructure ahead of its upcoming election, framing the incident in political terms. The pipeline carries gas critical to both countries.

The incident raises fresh concerns about energy security in Central Europe at a time when the region remains heavily dependent on Russian supply routes. No group has claimed responsibility. Investigators in both countries are treating it as a serious sabotage attempt.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline

world.headline: OPEC+ Boosts Output as Migrant Deaths Mount in Mediterranean
world.body:
OPEC+ agreed to increase oil production but cautioned that recovery from recent conflict-related disruptions to energy markets will be slow. The decision comes as fighting around the Strait of Hormuz threatens one of the world's most critical oil shipping lanes, adding pressure on global supply chains.

Separately, dozens of migrants are missing after a boat capsized near Libya in the Mediterranean. The incident is the latest in a string of deadly crossings as migration routes from North Africa remain active despite ongoing instability in the region.

sources.title: Dozens missing after boat carrying migrants capsizes near Libya
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
Dozens missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean

ai.headline: AI Music Tools Face Copyright Backlash From Artists
ai.body:
Suno, the AI music generator, is generating legal and ethical heat over its handling of copyrighted material. The Verge reports the platform's outputs closely mirror existing recordings, raising serious questions about how training data was sourced and whether artists consented. A separate musician has gone public claiming an AI company cloned her music and is now filing copyright claims against her - flipping the usual dynamic where artists sue platforms.

Together these cases signal that the music industry's legal reckoning with generative AI is accelerating. Unlike text or image disputes, music copyright has well-established precedent around melody and sound-alike recordings, giving artists stronger ground to stand on. Expect more litigation.

sources.title: Musician says AI company is cloning her music, filing claims against her
Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot Entertainment, Not a Reliable Tool
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now describe Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only" - a striking disclaimer for a product the company has aggressively marketed as a productivity tool embedded across Office, Windows, and enterprise software. TechCrunch flagged the language, which effectively shields Microsoft from liability if Copilot produces wrong or harmful outputs.

The framing puts Microsoft in an awkward position. It charges businesses real money for Copilot access while legally treating it as a novelty. This mirrors broader industry hedging, where AI companies tout transformative capability in marketing while quietly limiting accountability in the fine print. Users relying on Copilot for serious work should take note.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: Gemma 4 Arrives on iPhones and Local Machines
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is now running on iPhones via the AI Edge Gallery app, and developers are also deploying it locally using LM Studio's new headless CLI alongside Claude Code. The combination points to a maturing ecosystem for running capable models entirely on-device or on local hardware, without routing data through cloud APIs.

For privacy-conscious developers and enterprises, local inference has long been the goal. Gemma 4's arrival on consumer hardware suggests the performance gap between cloud and on-device models is narrowing faster than expected. One developer documented running it end-to-end in a workflow that would have required cloud infrastructure just months ago.

sources.title: Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code

ai.headline: Developers Push Back on AI-Generated Code in Open Source
ai.body:
A software developer has published a formal policy refusing AI-generated content in their open source projects, citing concerns about code quality, provenance, and the erosion of genuine understanding among contributors. The post resonates with a broader unease captured in another piece arguing that the real danger of AI coding tools is "comfortable drift" - developers shipping code they no longer fully understand.

These aren't fringe positions. As AI coding assistants become default tools, open source maintainers face a practical problem: reviewing contributions where the human author may not grasp what they submitted. The debate is sharpening around accountability, not just quality.

sources.title: Policy on adding AI generated content to my software projects
The threat is comfortable drift toward not understanding what you're doing

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K as Sentiment Craters Amid War Fears
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck around $67,000 with market sentiment at its worst levels since the Iran conflict began, and traders are calling new lows a matter of time. Polymarket briefly showed 63% odds of a US invasion of Iran before pulling controversial related prediction markets after public backlash - a sign of how raw the geopolitical mood is right now.

Despite the gloom, one structural shift is working in Bitcoin's favor. ETF-driven institutional money has changed how Bitcoin responds to macro signals - it now front-runs Federal Reserve moves rather than reacting to them, suggesting a more sophisticated investor base is setting the tone. A Bitcoin Policy Institute executive also argued this week that Bitcoin and the US dollar share a symbiotic relationship, pushing back on the zero-sum framing that dominates most macro commentary.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec

btc.headline: North Korea Pulled Off $270M Drift Hack Over Six Months
btc.body:
The $270 million exploit of crypto exchange Drift was not a smash-and-grab. The platform confirmed it was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation, pointing to a level of patience and sophistication that sets it apart from typical crypto hacks.

The attack lands as Ledger's CTO warns that AI is actively making crypto's security problem worse - lowering the bar for attackers to craft convincing phishing campaigns and automate vulnerability discovery. Quantum computing adds another layer of long-term threat, with new analysis explaining how advances in the field could eventually break the cryptographic foundations Bitcoin relies on. The security picture across the industry is deteriorating on multiple fronts simultaneously.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

btc.headline: Ant Group Bets on AI Agents Transacting Directly on Crypto Rails
btc.body:
Ant Group's blockchain division has launched a platform designed to let AI agents conduct transactions autonomously using cryptocurrency infrastructure. The move positions one of China's largest fintech players at the intersection of two of the most overhyped - and genuinely consequential - technology trends.

The practical bet here is that AI agents will need financial rails that don't require human intermediaries for every transaction, and that crypto infrastructure is better suited to that than traditional banking. It is an early-stage thesis, but Ant Group's scale gives it more weight than a typical startup pitch.

sources.title: Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails

btc.headline: Kiyosaki Revives 1974 Monetary Argument for Bitcoin and Gold
btc.body:
Robert Kiyosaki is back with a historical frame, arguing that the current moment mirrors the 1974 breakdown of the Bretton Woods system and that Bitcoin and gold are the logical response. The argument is familiar - fiat currency loses purchasing power over time, hard assets win - but Kiyosaki is pointing to a specific structural shift rather than just inflation anxiety.

The timing matters. With geopolitical tension elevated and sentiment in crypto at multi-month lows, the hard-money narrative has a receptive audience even if the messenger is polarizing.

sources.title: Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust shell filled with slow-roasted leeks lacquered in white miso and honey, topped with toasted walnuts.
food.tip: Salt the leeks and let them sit for 10 minutes before roasting — it draws out moisture and intensifies their sweetness dramatically.

exercise.name: The Sunday Locomotive
exercise.equipment: A park, open path, or quiet street — no gear needed
exercise.description: Alternate between 3 minutes of brisk walking, 90 seconds of skipping, and 60 seconds of walking lunges in a continuous loop, finishing with 5 minutes of slow walking and deliberate nasal breathing to wind down.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, and now engineered into medical devices and hearing aids.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start seeing its fingerprints in neuroscience, economics, and even how the brain processes ambiguous images.
